data.title: Flex: Adding To A Multi-Year Transition
data.description:
Shares of Flex Ltd. (FLEX) saw a big 6% pullback in response to what essentially is just a bolt-on deal for the business, albeit that the news coincided with a weak day for markets at large.

The recent deal fits the strategic rationale, that of becoming a higher-growth and higher-margin business, and thus it comes at a relatively expensive price. With shares falling by more than the acquisition price, it appears that the move in isolation is an overreaction, but this follows shares stretching their legs in recent years.

Hence, the near-term pullback looks interesting, but eventually, quite some achievements are priced in. Trading around 20 times realistic earnings here, with still a large cyclical element tied to the shares, I am erring on the side of caution, finding it too early to buy the dip just yet.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Adding Electrical Power Products

Flex has reached an agreement to acquire Electrical Power Products, a provider of engineered-to-order electrical power control and protection systems. The deal, valued at $1.1 billion, comes in at around $1.0 billion after factoring in anticipated tax benefits.

The Des Moines-based business designs, integrates, and manufactures highly engineered control and relay panels, as well as modular integrated control buildings for utility, power generation, and industrial applications. This makes this a highly strategic acquisition, one with a solid growth runway, driven by long-term trends around grid modernization, electrification, and data center build-outs.

Given a $323 million revenue contribution, the deal is valued at just over 3 times sales, with these sales growing at double digits on an organic basis. Adjusted EBITDA is seen in the mid-to-high teens, which suggests a roughly $50-$60 million EBITDA contribution, for a 17-20 times multiple paid on that front.

Deals like this fit the long-term strategy for Flex to move away from a relatively cyclical and low-margin contract manufacturer to proprietary products with higher margins, more stable demand, and better organic growth. Such efforts have been seen in previous dealmaking but also in new and high-end applications, with Flex announcing new solutions to accelerate AI deployment, among others.

A Smaller Contribution

Early in February, Flex reported its third-quarter results for its fiscal year 2026. Third quarter sales of $7.06 billion were up nearly 8%, as GAAP operating margins of 5.5% were up about forty basis points. Such margins are reported after modest restructuring and amortization charges are taken; these equal about 30 basis points.

Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.87, up some ten cents ahead of last year, yet this includes a $37 million stock-based compensation expense adjustment. That adjustment is equal to about $0.10 per share ahead of taxes, as earnings otherwise came in close to $0.80 per share.

The balance sheet was in excellent shape with net debt of $1.4 billion trailing EBITDA, and while pro forma net debt will jump to $2.5 billion, that is very manageable. Full-year sales are seen between $27.2 and $27.5 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around $3.24 per share, making realistic earnings come in just shy of $3 per share after we back out stock-based compensation expenses.

With 375 million shares trading at $60, Flex's equity is valued at $22.5 billion, or $23.9 billion ahead of the deal. This values the business at just 0.9 times sales and around 20 times earnings. In this light, recent acquisition activity takes place at elevated sales multiples and higher earnings multiples, but they improve the quality of Flex as a business.

However, the purchase of Electrical Power Products only adds just over 1% of sales, although it will add to margins and growth rates reported here, with the larger transition of Flex remaining a multi-year endeavor here.

Expectations Rise

During the 2010s, Flex was long considered a cyclical, volatile, and lower-margin contract manufacturer, with shares trading in the single digits. In fact, it was just a $15 stock early in 2023, as the rise of insourcing and related processes post the supply chain shock induced by the pandemic drove a massive rally in recent years. In fact, shares hit highs around $70 in December and recent weeks, now having seen a ten-dollar pullback, with shares now back to levels first seen in October of last year.

The dynamics are also in part reflected in the fundamental performance of the firm. In fact, revenues have only risen by about 10% over the past decade, thereby trailing inflation, as the real growth has been seen in terms of growing margins. Gross profits have risen by about 3-4 points to about 10% of sales, revealing massive gains as operating margins around 2-3% of sales have moved up to the mid-single digits.

The company has made some strategic deals in recent times to make such a transition happen. This includes the divestment of Nextpower (NXT), while the company announced a $325 million deal for Crown Technical Systems in 2024, accompanied by various other smaller deals, including that of JETCOOL Technologies.

Moreover, about a third of the share count has been bought back since this period of time. This makes it so that earnings per share have risen in a huge fashion despite flattish topline trends. However, the stock performance has outpaced these earnings per share improvements. This is largely because valuation multiples have increased over time, with investors getting more appreciative of Flex's performance here.

Supportive Stance

The reality is that I like the strategic direction that Flex is undergoing, becoming a better, more stable, higher-margin, and higher-growth business. However, deals like the one for Electrical Power Products really help to make this happen but are relatively small in their near-term contribution.

This makes it so that Flex has years and years of growth runway left, and investors would appreciate such abilities. However, trading close to 20 times earnings already, much of the re-rating potential is gone here.

Hence, I continue to have a constructive stance on Flex here, but getting involved, even after a recent ten-dollar pullback, is another story. With still a cyclical element to it, I need a larger earnings yield or a lower entry multiple to get enticed by the risk-reward proposition here.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Is The Stock Still Undervalued After The Rally? A Quantitative Approach
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I value Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) with a Hold judgment. The company is well-appreciated by investors for being one of the biggest and most diversified players in the pharmaceutical industry, a defensive stock with a long history (62 years) of dividend increase.

Given the considerable capital increase the company has been realizing in the last year, this analysis aims to quantitatively evaluate if the company is still undervalued, fairly valued, or overvalued. The outcome of the Monte Carlo DCF model implemented answers this question from a statistical and probabilistic point of view: the stock in the base case is overvalued in slightly more than 1 possible future out of 2.

This result has to be integrated with the analysis made on the stock’s risk-adjusted performance and on the stock’s quality relative to its competitors: JNJ ranks second in its peer group in terms of factor scorecard quality (only Eli Lily (LLY) does better), with the Fama-French regression analysis highlighting a structural break that happened after mid-2024 and the rolling Jensen’s alpha of the stock recovering after the litigation events.

Looking at the stock’s dividend performance, JNJ still has room to increase its dividends in the following year, holding a payout ratio of 64%, one of the lowest in its peer group. This characteristic makes the stock attractive for more defensive investors and contributes to justifying the hold rating. Nonetheless, mathematically, the dividend return per se is not sufficient to repay the investors for the risk taken in buying the stock; a capital increase is fundamental to bridge the gap of 1.13% between the equity return and the cost of equity of the stock.

Introduction and Business Overview

Johnson & Johnson is one of the largest and most famous companies in the pharmaceutical industry. Its specializations range from the production of pharmaceuticals and medical devices.

Before the Kenvue spinoff happened in 2023, the company used to have in its business model the consumer health division (with well-known brands such as Neutrogena). Today, the company’s business model relies on a pure-play pharmaceutical business (which accounts for roughly 65% of the company’s revenues) and the MedTech business, focused on the production of medical equipment for surgery and other medical applications (which accounts for ~35% of the company’s revenues).

Such business division is key to understanding both the orientation of the business and the metrics that I am going to analyze, as the pharmaceutical segment is the one driving both the ROIC and the margins of the company, while the MedTech division is a more diversified business, with higher cash flow predictability (as the products of the company do not depend on patent discovery and long legal approvals) and, of course, lower R&D intensity.

The company in the last years has been affected both operationally and financially by the talc litigation, which I identified in the following analysis as one (but not the only one) of the most impactful factors driving the rolling Fama-French alpha in the 2021-2024 period. It is not a coincidence, in my opinion, that the stock is now experiencing an evident alpha recovery as the direction of the legal dispute is heading to a settlement, involving the bankruptcy of one of JNJ's subsidiaries (LTL Management) and a settlement fund of ~$7 billion.

Methodology of the Analysis

I decided to produce a quantitative analysis to come up with a final judgment on the stock performance and on its rating. The analysis is going to include a first comparative part in which I am going to compare the risk-adjusted measures (Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, Calmar ratio, and Adj. Sharpe ratio) of JNJ across a group of six comparable companies: Eli Lilly (LLY), Merck & Co. (MRK), Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY), and Pfizer (PFE). This comparable group of peers has also been put in comparison with the returns and the performances of the market, here considered as the S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The second comparative part involves again the same peer group of companies, evaluated in this case with specific business and market metrics to assess the quality of JNJ’s business model and stock performances. Such comparative parts have been carried out using z-scores, allowing for statistical reliability of the comparison.

The third part is focused on the Fama-French regression to really understand what the real drivers of the stock’s market performance are and if it is possible to evaluate the Jensen’s alpha of the stock itself.

The fourth part is partially based on the third one, and it is focused on the valuation of the stock, using a probabilistic approach centered on a Monte Carlo DCF to derive possible scenarios for the intrinsic value of JNJ. After that, the analysis focuses on the dividend approach of the company, well-known for its dividend distribution history.

For the analysis I considered 1,816 trading days across 7 tickers, covering roughly 7.2 years. This choice has been made to make my statistical estimates as reliable as possible, as they will be the fundamental factors of the study. All the financial data of the different stocks analyzed (both JNJ and the peer group, S&P 500 included) are taken from Yahoo Finance.

Evaluation of Risk-Adjusted Measures

Using the Sharpe Ratio (which adjusts for skewness and excess kurtosis), I came up with the following ranking of risk-adjusted returns: LLY, SPY, JNJ, MRK, ABT, BMY, PFE. Such ranking appears to remain stable also across the other index used for the analysis, and this is a strong stability signal, as it indicates that the performances of the risk-adjusted measures do not depend on the methodology used to measure them: the hierarchical performance is clear and robust in the peer group.

LLY is surprising in the performance comparison, as the stock dominates the other comparable in all the risk-adjusted measures: the Calmar ratio of 1.009 is exceptional, as it means that the stock delivered more than $1 of annualized return for every $1 of max drawdown, and the upward trend is probably due to the GLP-1 obesity drug effects. One important remark, though: this exceptional performance is slightly obscured by the 13% difference between LLY’s Sharpe Ratio and its Adj. Sharpe Ratio. If we consider the positive skewness and the kurtosis in the distribution (indicating the potential for occasional large gains and the presence of large extreme events in the stock performance, respectively), the Sharpe Ratio of the stock declines significantly.

JNJ stock performance and risk-adjusted metrics are different in multiple aspects from LLY. Firstly, the adjusted Sharpe Ratio is not significantly different from the Sharpe Ratio itself, and, in general, the adjustment is the least punishing among all the stocks, meaning that the non-normality of the stock’s returns is not a case for the company. Secondly, Sharpe ratios (both classic and adjusted) are very similar to the Sortino ratio, which considers only the negative volatility (essentially, only the volatility caused by negative trading days of the stock), and such a case is interesting, as it gives us evidence that the stock’s upside and downside volatility are essentially symmetric and extreme events, such as the litigation case, produced more uncertain days than directional crushes for the stock. But the most important insight is the last one: the Calmar ratio of the stock is higher than the Adjusted Sharpe ratio and the Sortino ratio, which is the opposite of S&P 500 and LLY. It means that the maximum drawdown of the stock was relatively contained if we compare it with its day-to-day volatility; in other words, despite the normal noise and day-to-day volatility, the cumulative damage related to sustained negative days was relatively contained with respect to the index or the biggest competitor, and that’s evidence of a defensive stock.

While MRK, ABT, and BMY work as a middle ground and comparable peers (especially for the median metric) without anything so interesting to highlight, it’s worth taking a look at the only stock with negative metrics in the peer group: PFE. The stock has both a negative Adj. Sharpe ratio, meaning that it generates negative excess return (an investor would have been better off investing in Treasury Bills), and a negative, but near-zero Calmar ratio: despite the negative annualized returns, the maximum drawdown (the worst-case scenario happened) was so large as to reduce the ratio to almost zero, highlighting how the stock fell from the top without having generated enough return to compensate for this fall (yet).

Going deeper into the peer metrics analysis and looking at the rolling Sharpe ratio, we can get more insights on the stock’s risk-adjusted returns by considering the time dimension. For the analysis, we used a time-varying risk-free rate, pulling out the daily effective Federal Funds rate from the FED FRED database. After having shown its defensive nature during the 2022 market crush (for the rising rates), JNJ suffered from the litigation events delivering negative returns (blue shaded area in the chart) while the market and most competitors were recovering. But starting from the beginning of 2026, the stock headed towards positive territory, reaching above 2.0. Currently, the stock is generating its best risk-adjusted returns in seven years.

All the analyses made so far on JNJ suggest (but do not prove) that the underperformance of the stock was more event-driven than structural. The stock itself behaves like a defensive stock, but we still need to question whether this defensive stock is expensive or not.

Factor Score Valuation

So far we have analyzed the past performance of the stock using backward-looking ratios; the point of the analysis is now to combine the ratios used into a more fundamental-driven study of the stock. To achieve this, we built different key business metrics for JNJ, and we compared them to the selected peer universe using z-scores. The statistical use of the z-score is key in this type of analysis to ensure that the comparison of each metric is made on the same scale. We compared the companies using the following metrics:

  • Owner earnings yield, which is a cash-based yield computed as net income + D&A - CapEx. Much preferable to EV/EBITDA because it enables the comparison between companies with different CapEx intensity.
  • Adjusted ROIC, considering R&D expenses as capital expenditures
  • R&D/Revenues, measuring the intensity of the innovation activities of the companies
  • FCF CAGR, preferred to the classic revenue CAGR, as we’re talking about mature businesses in which cash flow performance matters more than top-line growth
  • Net Debt/Owner Earnings
  • Momentum, to account for market signals. We deliberately excluded the return of the last month to correct for the short-term reversal effect of the stock, as demonstrated by Jegadeesh and Titman (1993).
  • Short ratio to include another market signal. The ratio has been computed as shares sold short divided by average trading volume in a day, with a ratio above 5 days that can be interpreted as evidence of diffuse bearish sentiment of investors towards the stock.

The results of the analysis show how JNJ is z-score neutral on OE yield, meaning that even after the litigation events, the company does not seem to trade cheap relative to its cash generation. Regarding the short interest, the positive score obtained by JNJ shows that the company doesn’t have much short interest compared to its peers, which are more focused on the negative performance of MRK and PFE, especially.

Good scores for JNJ are the net debt/OE, which shows a solid balance sheet structure, with a leveraging and debt structure almost one standard deviation below the peer group, and the momentum, which highlights the reward given by the market to the stock (confirming the positive returns that we saw in the rolling Sharpe ratio chart).

It is interesting looking at two fundamental scores that we consider key for pharmaceutical business: the adjusted ROIC, which considers R&D expenses (at the core of a typical pharma company) as CapEx, and the R&D on Revenue ratio. After capitalizing for R&D expenses, JNJ's ROIC ranks second in the peer group at 0.86 standard deviations above the peer mean. Just LLY is performing better, with a metric of 1.52 standard deviations above the group’s mean, reflecting the surprising returns of the GLP-1 drug investments.

The R&D/revenue ratio is actually negative for JNJ, meaning that the spending on research activities of the company is 0.30 standard deviation below the peer group: JNJ is spending less than the comparable companies in what is considered the core of the pharmaceutical business. However, such circumstances can have two different interpretations, one negative and one positive:

  • The bearish one: Lower R&D expenses mean less innovation today, which leads to a smaller pipeline tomorrow, lower growth, and more modest returns
  • The bullish one: Lower R&D expenses with a high efficiency (demonstrated by the higher ROIC) are more important and more significant than the absolute value of these spendings

We should also consider the MedTech division of JNJ, which is a significant part of the business model and generally requires less R&D expenses than a pure pharmaceutical business (in MedTech the development cycles for machines are objectively shorter and less capital intensive than the research activities for discovering a new drug).

Which interpretation is more appropriate to choose is a personal investing choice based on intuition, knowledge, and sentiment. Personally, I tend to favor the bullish one, as, at the end of the day, what an investor is looking for are returns, and JNJ not only is the second company in the group with the highest returns but can also reach them with fewer financial resources employed in R&D.

Below we included the color-coded scorecard for the companies in the peer group, summing up all the considerations made so far.

Fama-French Regression and Factor Decomposition

After having looked at the risk-return metrics and after having described how JNJ looks compared to its peers through the factor scorecard, I performed a Fama-French regression to understand what the primary drivers of JNJ’s stock returns are and if (and what) alpha the business has generated. The data used are taken from Ken French's public database.

I considered six key factors:

  • MKT_RF, which corresponds to the market beta. How does JNJ respond to market movements
  • SMB, which accounts for the company size and measures the returns differently between a sample of small companies and a sample of large companies
  • HML, which tells us whether the stock behaves like a value or growth stock
  • RMW, which is a factor explaining the behavior of the stock when profitable companies tend to outperform low-profit companies
  • CMA, a factor indicating the company’s behavior in terms of a conservative or aggressive capital allocator
  • MOM, highlighting how a company responds to momentum. Does it tend to go up after a positive period or vice versa?

After having controlled for these factors, the returns that remain unexplained can be considered as the alpha of the company.

The output of my performed regression is as follows:

One fundamental technical note: the regression performed is an OLS regression with Heteroskedasticity and Autocorrelation between standard errors. Such methodology has been used to correctly address the behavior of daily stock returns, which violates both the assumption of Homoskedasticity (since, under GARCH effects, we can observe volatility clusters in stocks’ performances) and independence of errors.

Starting with the interpretation of each factor:

MKT_RF is positive (loading of +0.47) and statistically significant (p-value indistinguishable from zero); such a result confirms JNJ’s defensive characteristic as a stock: when the market goes up 1%, the stock responds by going up by just 0.47%, holding other factors constant.

SMB is negative (loading of -0.28) and statistically significant, an expected result that tells us how JNJ tends to underperform in periods in which small companies outperform large-cap companies. Being JNJ, one of the largest US pharmaceutical companies, this metric is coherent.

HML is slightly positive but statistically insignificant at a p-value=1%. This is an interesting result that tells us how JNJ does not really perform either as a value stock or a growth stock, ranging somewhere in the middle. This circumstance can partially be explained by the nature of pharma companies, as their performances are strongly tied to patent discovery.

RMW is positive (loading of +0.13) and statistically significant at p-value = 5%. When high-profit companies outperform low-profit companies, JNJ participates in the outperformance. Again, this is a market result confirming the high-quality metrics characterizing the company that we saw above in the comparison with the peer group.

CMA is also strongly positive (loading of 0.38, the second highest after the beta) and statistically significant. The regression output confirms JNJ’s nature as a conservative capital allocator, which is coherent with its dividend-oriented and controlled acquisition strategy

Finally, MOM is slightly negative and statistically significant: JNJ behaves slightly as an anti-momentum stock, which also confirms JNJ's defensive characteristic. When pro-momentum strategies and companies fail, JNJ tends to improve its performance. On the contrary, the stock is more inclined to underperform when momentum stocks continue improving.

The alpha, on an annualized measure, is 0.87%, but it has an astronomically high p-value of 0.88, which makes its interpretation and validity totally insignificant from a statistical point of view, as the uncertainty around the measure is too high to conclude anything about it: we know that there is an alpha, but we cannot estimate it with certainty.

It’s worth looking at the month-by-month trajectory of both alpha and beta measures. In fact, even if we cannot tell with statistical certainty if JNJ’s alpha is really different from zero, due to great noise signals relative to the sample period considered, we can still verify if the stock’s alpha has systematically varied over time and by how much.

The charts above explain it. The first one illustrates the 12-month Fama-French factor alpha for JNJ, with the confidence intervals highlighted with different shades of blue. It is clear that we cannot obtain the point estimate of the alpha due to the high signal-to-noise bounds, but the graph gives us a sense of possible paths. The bottom chart shows the t-statistics of the estimates in the 12-month analysis. We can see that the statistic never reaches the minimum threshold of 2.0 (the minimum threshold to get statistical significance in the point estimates), but the direction of this statistic, which went from steadily negative to positive from early 2025, suggests that the litigation discount applied by the market to JNJ stock during 2023-2024 is currently unwinding. Such a structural break is also confirmed by the Chow Test:

We have strong statistical evidence that the factor model has changed regime from mid-2024.

I wanted to investigate the same Fama-French 6-Factor Loading as well for the peer group of 6 companies that we analyzed above, and the results, reported in the table below, were without any doubt interesting.

Firstly, the only company with a significant positive alpha over the considered time period is LLY (+23.1% with statistical significance at p-value=5%). This means that the factor model could not explain or anticipate the drug discovery in the diabetes treatment, and this discovery enabled the company to generate unexpected returns that we can quantify solidly thanks to their high magnitude.

Another interesting discovery is that JNJ behaves as the most defensive stock in the group (a group of overall defensive stocks), thanks to the lowest beta; at the same time, JNJ is a unique case for its profitability factor (RMW), as the company has the only single statistically significant positive number: JNJ is the only company in the peer group to have stock returns linked to the outperformance of high-profitability stocks.

The analysis made so far lacks a final valuation question that needs to be addressed. I will try to estimate the true value of the stock through a probabilistic approach, computing a Monte Carlo DCF simulation.

Monte Carlo DCF Valuation

I consider the classic DCF approach for valuation and estimation of the target price as limited, as the key inputs for the valuation (terminal growth rate, WACC, and FCF Margin) are essentially unknown in the future, and therefore, any punctual estimation derived by them is, in my opinion, too imprecise and uncertain.

Uncertainty is something that cannot be eliminated when projecting estimates in the future, but to address it in what I believe is one of the best ways possible, I decided to estimate a DCF using a Monte Carlo simulation. In this way it is possible to model uncertainty by computing several thousand times every input in a range of possible assumptions, with the final goal of reporting a distribution of the output rather than a single point estimate.

I estimated the DCF on a 5-year horizon to limit as much as possible the problem of visibility in revenue and margin

For what concerns assumptions, starting with revenue growth, I decided to use a 5.62% growth for year 1, derived from analyst consensus, linearly declining such growth rate from year 2 to year 5 to the terminal growth rate. Since the terminal growth rate will be chosen randomly in each Monte Carlo simulation, each simulation will have its own revenue path.

For the FCF Margin, I decided to use the median value of the last 4 fiscal years (20.69%). This decision is particularly supported by the fact that JNJ showed historical stability in this metric (starting from 2022: 21.5%, 20.9%, 20.3%, 20.5%); this circumstance avoided me having to elaborate on a more sophisticated method to model the rate. For simulation purposes, I assumed the FCF Margin to follow a Beta distribution (with bounds at [0, 1])

The terminal growth rate is derived from a truncated normal distribution centered at 2.5%, with limits at 2.0% and 3.0%, two scenarios that assume, respectively, a lower and a higher expected inflation.

For WACC estimation, I remained consistent with the use of the Fama-French factor model. Admitting the limits of the CAPM formula, I estimated the cost of equity of the company using the five factor loadings (the momentum factor was excluded as it is not considered a contributor to the fundamental systematic risk) and the factor risk premia realized historically, taken from Ken French’s database. Moreover, again for simulation purposes, I assumed the WACC to follow a log-normal distribution with zero as a lower bound

I also considered the statistical dilemma behind the Monte Carlo method: using the simple Monte Carlo methodology to build the DCF would have required the assumption of treating FCF Margin, WACC, and terminal growth rate as independent variables with respect to each other, but it’s obvious that large economic events (such as high inflation periods) highlight the correlation between these variables (WACC is higher and puts more pressure on FCF Margin, which affects long-run growth). Assuming independence would underestimate the probability of the realization of extreme negative results.

So, I decided to introduce a t-copula to address these co-movements of the inputs, which required me to express the correlation matrix between FCF Margin, WACC, and terminal growth rate. The results can be observed in the picture below:

The correlation between FCF Margin and WACC is negative (as expected), but moderate, highlighting how an increase in the discount rate (due to, for example, an increase in the risk-fee rate) compresses the margins; the correlation between FCF and terminal growth rate is positive, as an economic growth eventually enables the company to receive benefits in its cash margins and the relationship between WACC and terminal growth rate is positive as underlined by the Fisher equation: nominal rates, which implies an higher WACC, are directly linked to nominal growth.

The outcomes of the Monte Carlo DCF simulation are shown in the picture below:

I assumed the WACC in the bear case at 7.00%, as obtained using the Fama-French method, while I considered in the base case a WACC of 6.31%, which is the result of the average between the FF WACC and the WACC implied by the market’s current valuation of the stock (~5.62%).

  • In the bear case, the stock is overvalued in more than 3 plausible futures out of 4, with a median value for the intrinsic share price of $202 and an overvaluation of 19.8%.
  • In the base case, the stock seems to be fairly valued, with a median intrinsic value of $242, with the probability of being undervalued of almost 50% (in almost 1 plausible future out of 2, the stock is undervalued).

The overall valuation for JNJ stock, using the probabilistic DCF approach, is then a Hold rating.

Dividend Valuation

As JNJ is considered a dividend-heavy company with a long history of dividend increases, it’s worth looking at how it has performed, also relative to the peer group, and what the possible scenarios are for the future.

The picture above shows how JNJ has performed relative to its comparable companies.

The current dividend yield sits at 2.14%, with an FCF payout ratio of 64%, signaling space for the company to further increase its dividend reward to investors. PFE is at the top in terms of dividend yield but also at the top in terms of payout ratio, with an unsustainable level of 107.7%: the company is paying in dividends more than what it is generating in cash flows.

Looking at the Dividend Growth Rate, JNJ shows a lower-than-average 3Y CAGR and a more sustained 5Y CAGR, nothing special for the company, but the compound growth rate is consistent, and I expect it to remain around 3.5-4% on 5Y. The outlier in this case is, obviously, LLY, which was able to increase its dividend returns thanks to the great economic returns generated by the GLP-1 revenue.

It is also worth looking at the Gordon Growth Model gap. Having computed the cost of equity using the Fama-French method (in the Monte Carlo DCF section), we can see if the current dividend yield plus the 5Y dividend CAGR is alone sufficient to compensate investors for the equity risk of the company. This is not the case for JNJ, as the Gordon Implied return is 1.13 percentage points lower than what has been estimated as the equity risk of the company. I wouldn’t look at this case as a negative and a sell case, but if an investor is thinking of buying the stock convinced that the (expected, given the present and the past) dividend returns are sufficient to repay the risk he (or she) has to bear, then this is not the case. A capital increase of the stock is required to bridge the current gap of 1.13%.

Risks and Limits

There is one important limit of this valuation that must be highlighted: the Monte Carlo DCF values the company using only its cash flow profile. There is no mathematical value or probability assigned to new drug discovery, pharmaceutical pipelines, or MedTech innovations other than what is already considered in the growth rates applied. Obviously, these parameters matter, but a quantification of them (assuming that it’s possible) would require assumptions on fundamental drivers and personal valuations, which are behind the objective of this analysis.

Similarly, I need to underline the fact that the litigation is not yet fully resolved and the DCF model computed is not considering possible negative events for the company tied to the legal dispute, as their impact on cash flows and growth is difficult to quantify.

Conclusion

Overall, I rate Johnson & Johnson as a Hold. The company is one of the most stable and performative companies in the pharmaceutical sector. It is obviously lagging players like Eli Lily, which is finding huge results thanks to its GLP-1 revenues, but it is performing well on multiple metrics such as the R&D Adj. ROIC, the cash flow generation, and the momentum. The Fama-French regression confirms that Johnson & Johnson is one of those big companies that find their strength in profitability, behaving like a traditional defensive stock.

The Monte Carlo DCF simulation highlighted that the stock is currently correctly priced by the market, potentially slightly overvalued from a probabilistic point of view, which is enough to avoid putting a buy rating on it, but not enough to conclude the analysis with a bearish call.

data.title: Rio Tinto: Fundamentals Don't Support A Price Rally
data.description:
Since I last wrote about the multi-commodity miner Rio Tinto Group (RIO) a little over a year ago, the stock is up by 40%. And this is despite the fact that it has lost some 12% of its value from the pre-war highs.

I hadn't foreseen this increase then, not even with its not-too-bad dividend yield, not even for the medium term. Yet, here we are. What changed? That's the question to address here. Following which, I consider whether RIO can continue to see an uptick this year.

Why Has Rio Tinto Risen Over The Past Year?

While some of RIO's rise over the past year was sentiment-driven, it was also increasingly based in improving fundamentals, as discussed in the three key reasons for the rise below:

#1. Iron Ore Uptick Mid-Year

At the time of my last article, the prospects for iron ore prices were underwhelming. This was a key challenge for RIO, as iron ore brings in half of the company's underlying EBITDA. The price forecasts weren't wrong either, eventually, with a 20% YoY decline observed in average price over 2025.

However, after touching a low for the year in June 2025, iron ore prices started inching upwards on improved Chinese demand. Along with this, RIO started seeing a cautious rise as well as sentiment around the stock improved.

#2. Copper And Aluminium Offer Support

Copper and aluminium, which bring in much of the remainder of adjusted EBITDA, also supported RIO with rising prices. The average price of copper rose by 25% in 2025, and aluminium prices saw a 15% rise.

By H1 2025, both segments had already seen massive increases in underlying EBITDA. Copper saw a rise of 60% YoY, and aluminium grew by 59% YoY. Even though the total figure for Rio Tinto contracted by 5%, owing to a 24% drop in iron ore's underlying EBITDA, they did help in softening the decline.

By the end of 2025, so much momentum was gained for copper that the overall underlying EBITDA actually expanded by 9% (see table below). The total figure was also helped by the fact that the extent of contraction for iron ore cooled off to 11% for the full year. The aluminium segment, which got consolidated with lithium after the completion of the company's acquisition of Arcadium Lithium, reported a smaller but still strong 29% increase in underlying EBITDA too. The rise compared to H1 2025 was reduced on account of the US tariffs.

#3. Sales Expand After Three Years

In arguably the most positive development, the company's full-year revenues expanded once again, and by a healthy 7%. This is a massive positive after they contracted for three consecutive years up to 2024. This was a markedly different outcome from the 4.1% contraction forecast as per analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha when I last checked, presumably on improved prospects after that time.

While the company's attributable post-tax profits continued to drop significantly, by 14%, even this contraction was smaller than the ~18% drop expected when I last wrote in February 2025.

Mixed Segment-Wise Indications For 2026

The next question is whether Rio Tinto's fundamentals can strengthen further this year. Let's first look at the prospects from a segment-wise perspective.

  • Iron ore

The company's guidance (see table below) indicates that iron ore is certainly expected to see higher sales, of 3.7%, at the midpoint of the guidance range. So far in 2026, iron ore prices haven't done too badly either, even as they saw a massive drop in February.

Since then, positive indications from China, risk premium due to the war, and some concerns about supplies have resulted in a recovery in prices to the point that they are no different from where they started the year. However, over the course of the year, the prospects might not be as good owing to oversupply. Basically, the iron ore story could go either way.

  • Copper

Copper's prospects, too, don't look encouraging. To start with, even at the top end of the guidance range, copper production is expected to fall short of that of last year. Copper prices are seeing a slight pullback from where they started the year as well. The outlook for the remainder of the year is weak too, based on adequate supply and reduced Chinese demand. Based on current indications, then, investors should brace for a possible fall in copper revenues and possibly profits too.

  • Aluminium

Aluminium however, could be the standout segment for Rio Tinto this year. Not only do production prospects look essentially stable for it, but the metal has seen a 16% price rise YTD as well. In fact, it's set to report the biggest monthly gains since April 2018.

While a protracted war can continue to keep aluminium prices elevated in the foreseeable future, how far the rise continues remains to be seen. Also, keeping in mind aluminium's relatively small contribution to profits, even significant gains might not be able to support earnings completely if iron ore and copper see weakness. Still, for now, the segment's evolution over the course of 2026 is something to look forward too.

Outlook And Market Multiples

Despite reasons for concern, though, analysts estimates on Seeking Alpha provide a whole lot of comfort. Revenue growth is expected to essentially sustain from last year, rising by 6.4%, while EPS growth is seen at a massive 21.5%. Even this cheerful outlook doesn't help, however.

The stock's forward non-GAAP P/E at 10.9x is higher than the five-year average ratio of 8.2x, suggesting a correction ahead. And if the EPS estimates are downgraded further into the year, the price correction would be bigger.

There's something to be said about RIO's dividends, though. At 5.7%, the forward dividend yield is head and shoulders above the corresponding yield for the materials sector at 1.8%. It's even more heartening as the company has a long history of maintaining dividend payments.

What's Next?

It's possible that RIO could continue to rise if prices of its key commodities stay strong. But that's a gamble driven by sentiment and, hopefully, temporary upsets to supply. From a demand-supply perspective, iron ore prices should correct. Copper revenues could drop as well, though aluminium can offer some support.

Next, while earnings estimates are still highly positive, market valuations still indicate a correction ahead. In other words, no matter which way RIO is considered, caution is called for. I'm retaining a Hold rating.

data.title: Franklin Mutual International Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: International stock markets, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, advanced in US dollar terms in the fourth quarter of 2025. Optimism about the benefits of stronger European economic growth stemming from increased German fiscal spending helped boost regional stocks. Japanese equities, meanwhile, benefited from increased domestic spending and ongoing corporate reform efforts.
  • Contributors: Stock selection and an overweight in information technology, a lack of exposure to real estate and stock selection in consumer discretionary bolstered relative results. Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), Metso (OUKPF) and AerCap (AER) were among the individual contributors to relative performance.
  • Detractors: Security selection in industrials, financials and consumer staples curbed relative returns. Individual detractors included Tencent (TCEHY), Mondelez International (MDLZ) and RENK Group (RNKGF).
  • Outlook: We remain focused on identifying opportunities to acquire stocks at attractive valuations based upon our assessment of fundamental value. Our process of finding underappreciated and misunderstood companies with identifiable catalysts to unlock shareholder value may provide meaningful upside potential and possible downside risk management during turbulent periods.

Performance Review

  • The Fund (Class Z without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Value Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.
  • South Korean technology company Samsung Electronics contributed to relative results following solid earnings, optimism about a rebound in the memory chip cycle, positive news around an arrangement with artificial intelligence firm OpenAI (OPENAI), and expectations it will qualify at NVIDIA (NVDA) for high-bandwidth memory. The positive business momentum has led to upgraded 2026 earnings forecasts.
  • Finnish mining equipment company Metso contributed to relative results following a strong quarterly report, supported by strong orders, resilient margins and improving working capital. We continue to believe that the market has not given the company enough credit for a portfolio overhaul that has made the business less cyclical and more focused on the attractive copper and gold markets.
  • China-based gaming, online advertising and fintech company Tencent curbed relative results after a strong third quarter. Tencent's dominant position in China across gaming, advertising/communication, and fintech, and its investments companies across generative artificial intelligence and gaming leave it well placed to benefit from monetization opportunities as well as potential disruption.
  • Food and beverage manufacturer Mondelez International weighed on relative results, as financial results for its latest quarter came in below expectations with profit margins falling on higher commodity costs. Results in Europe were affected by a hot summer and Mondelez saw weaker consumer demand in North America. We believe the company will be able to return to growth as it navigates higher costs.

Outlook

  • Plans for increased competitiveness across the European Union are supporting spending, employment and growth. We expect German fiscal stimulus to feed through to the broader domestic and European economies in 2026. Not only will Germany and other European countries spend more on defense, but they also have begun to invest in a wider range of civilian infrastructure, which can benefit value industries focused on building materials, roads, rails and the like.
  • Eurozone unemployment continues to sit near a record low. The European Central Bank expects strong consumer and government spending, improving financing conditions and increased foreign demand to support economic growth within the region in the coming year. However, we still expect lingering political instability in France and budget uncertainty in both France and the United Kingdom to serve as a modest near-term headwind to an already floundering economy.
  • The United States' pullback from NATO has raised the risks of continued geopolitical uncertainty. As the world adjusts to new trade relationships, alienation of traditional US allies and a retooling of global trade are resulting in a small but increasing portion of global trade being conducted in currencies other than the US dollar, which may result in reduced demand for the dollar. Other things being equal, a weakening dollar can boost the return of non-dollar denominated assets.
  • In Japan, the drive to improve governance continues, and the economy continues to strengthen slowly. The Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE) is encouraging companies to improve their returns in a push to make the country more investable. Organizations with clear plans to improve returns and margins while reducing their cost of capital, and that are willing to buy back stock trading below book value following the unwinding of cross-shareholdings, are particularly appealing for more intense analysis, in our opinion.
  • We remain focused on identifying opportunities to acquire stocks at attractive valuations based upon our assessment of fundamental value. Our process of finding underappreciated and misunderstood companies with identifiable catalysts to unlock shareholder value may provide meaningful upside potential and possible downside risk management during turbulent periods.
