data.title: ServiceNow: A Strong Bet On Agentic AI
data.description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) is a leading enterprise-focused Cloud platform that offers customers a path to participate in the growth of the agentic AI market. With the utilization of artificial intelligence, more and more workflows in the enterprise market are going to get automated in the future, which creates a huge lever for the SaaS enterprise to leverage its core services. Further, ServiceNow announced earlier this year that its board authorized a new $5.0B stock buyback, and the company, given a shift towards AI-driven platform revenues, is poised to see higher operating margins in the future as well.

The recent consolidation in the SaaS market, driven by fears over the disruptive impact of AI on software companies, is a big opportunity for investors to back up the truck here, in my opinion.

Strong agentic AI market opportunity to boost margin expansion and top-line growth

ServiceNow is a dominant force in the enterprise software market as it offers its customers a unified AI-powered platform designed to digitize and automate business processes. Its core value proposition lies in the Now Platform, which serves as a "platform of platforms" that consolidates the four following workflows: IT Service Management, Customer Service Management, Employee Experience and Creator Workflows.

Most importantly, ServiceNow offers customers the opportunity to harness the power of artificial intelligence by tapping into the explosively growing agentic AI market. Agentic AI allows customers to utilize and deploy autonomous AI agents to complete pre-defined tasks in order to streamline work processes and improve enterprise productivity. The agentic AI industry, and therefore ServiceNow, is set for rapid expansion as AI technologies get rolled out at scale in the enterprise market.

In the fourth fiscal quarter, ServiceNow’s growth highlighted growing demand for its core platform services, especially with regard to its AI products. Artificial intelligence-driven products on the ServiceNow platform, such as 'Now Assist'—the firm’s generative AI engine—have become primary catalysts for contract wins in 2025. ServiceNow, for instance, expanded its relationship with AI GPU maker Nvidia (NVDA) in order to co-develop native AI Agents and enterprise-grade AI Factories. It also signed a deal with IT solutions provider Carahsoft in March to roll out its AI products to the public sector, which creates upside potential in the firm's revenues and gross profits.

In Q4 ’25, ServiceNow published subscription revenues of $3.5B, showing 21% year-over-year growth. For the full year, ServiceNow posted $12.9B in total revenues, highlighting a year-over-year growth rate of 21% as well.

While the company's top line is surging, ServiceNow has a good chance to benefit from growing AI adoption in both private and public markets, in the sense that it could grow its gross profit margins. As a leading SaaS franchise, ServiceNow is currently leading in terms of gross profit margins in its industry group: it published a gross margin of ~77% in the last quarter, which makes the SaaS enterprise even more profitable (from a gross margin point of view) than key competitor Salesforce (CRM). I have repeatedly covered Salesforce and have a Strong Buy rating on the CRM applications provider due to its aggressive stock buybacks, Agentforce momentum, and strong profitability in the SaaS market: AI Disruption Fears Create Mispricing.

The agentic AI market represents a strong business and growth opportunity for ServiceNow, and the market is just about to take off if projections made by Precedence Research, a market intelligence firm focused on the deployment of AI, are correct: the research platform estimates that the agentic AI market is going to expand to a volume of more than $199B by the end of 2034, which implies a massive 26.4X factor increase over the 2025 base year. This calculates to an average annual market expansion rate of 44%.

ServiceNow: 50%+ discount to historical P/E

In 2026, software technology companies as well as private credit companies that extended loans primarily to SaaS/software platforms have seen a serious downward repricing of their shares due to growing fears over the broad-scale utilization of AI. The fear of AI-driven replacement of software companies, in my opinion, is massively exaggerated, as companies like ServiceNow actually benefit from growing AI adoption in their core markets.

Further, EPS growth trends indicate potential for consistent growth.

ServiceNow has the highest estimated EPS growth in the years ahead—standing at ~25% annually—which means the SaaS enterprise is projected to expand even faster than high-growth plays like Oracle (ORCL). Oracle is currently seeing a massive ramp in its Cloud business, which resulted in investors pricing in ~20% annual earnings growth. Salesforce, which is aggressively penetrating the agentic AI market with its own platform-specific Agentforce offer, is estimated to grow its earnings per share at ~18% going forward.

Since AI, in my opinion, is more of an opportunity than a risk for large SaaS firms that run scalable platform businesses, I believe the downturn in the valuation multiplier creates a contrarian buying opportunity. Additionally, earlier this month ServiceNow announced it struck a collaboration with government contractor Carahsoft to bring the platform's AI products to the public sector. This comes on top of the company's announcement earlier this year that a $5.0B stock buyback program was authorized by ServiceNow's board of directors.

Currently, shares of ServiceNow are trading at just ~21X forward (FY 2027) earnings, which is just about half of what they cost on average in the last three years. The main reason for ServiceNow's valuation downturn, as explained above, is a major souring of investor sentiment with regard to software platforms.

However, the actual financial performance of ServiceNow—specifically with regard to top-line and gross profit expansion—highlights a healthy growth setup for investors, and there are no visible cracks in the company's business model. I therefore see no reason why shares of ServiceNow could not reprice to a ~40X P/E given the firm's strong growth prospects linked to the agentic AI market... especially if enterprise-driven AI adoption continues to soar. A 40.0X earnings multiplier, which reconciles to ServiceNow's 3-year average P/E, calculates to a fair value for the SaaS platform's shares of $201 per share (+91% upside potential).

Risks with ServiceNow

The biggest risk for ServiceNow, as I see it, relates to a potential slowdown in the expansion rate of AI rollout plans in the hyperscaler market. If hyperscalers and enterprise customers came to the conclusion that AI was not as monetizable as initially thought, the software sector could face more serious headwinds.

However, so far AI has been a catalyst for growing subscription revenues for ServiceNow and other agentic AI-focused software platforms, like Salesforce. What would get me to change my mind about the SaaS enterprise is if the company were to see a contraction in its gross margins or suffer a drop of its subscription growth rate below 20% annually.

Final thoughts

ServiceNow is focused on the agentic AI market and is seeing rapid AI product uptake in its core business... all of which is fueling growth in subscription revenues. The continual automation of enterprise workflows suggests that the agentic AI market could explode in volume over the next decade, which would obviously support the platform’s top line and gross profit growth.

I like ServiceNow's valuation multiplier compression in a market that has seen considerable fear and uncertainty lately, indicating that investors may currently not rationally assess the software sector's long-term growth potential. With shares of ServiceNow trading at only 21X earnings, while 20% annual EPS growth rates are projected going forward, I see a strong contrarian investment backdrop for investors that believe in the potency of agentic AI technologies and continual demand for workflow streamlining in the enterprise market.

data.title: Visa: From Defensive To Compounding Opportunity After Valuation Reset
data.description:
This is a follow-up Buy call on Visa (V) from the Buy I issued in July last year. The long-term retirement asset categorization still stays valid. Operational performance has not been a drag since my Buy call. However, the stock has failed to live up to the lower drawdown positioning I had claimed in July 2025 - and that is one of the core changes to the thesis from July. While Visa stayed flat during the bull run in the rest of the markets since July last year, it has corrected like a growth stock in the late 2025 to the current sell-off. The net effect is a ~15% drawdown in a period where the broader markets (proxied by the S&P 500) have been flat on an end-to-end basis.

There are some concerns around normalized growth expectations and a potentially mix-related margin pressure regime that could explain this drawdown and should be on investors' watchlists too. However, the net effect, in my view, is that this changes the positioning from a defensive stabilizer to a high-quality compounder. And the Buy rating is retained, looking at how the valuation reset has aligned Visa closer to the expected normalized growth profile with an eye on the mix.

Original Positioning and Why the Drawdown

The original Buy thesis relied on Visa's defensive-like lower drawdown profile, supported by a secular and stable tollbooth-like business model. Visa's business also exhibits a low GDP linkage (inflation pass-through via payments). Tailwinds from digital payments are supportive for the long-term thesis. FCF remains strong with margins around the 55% mark, and buybacks since July alone account for close to a 1% reduction in share count (EPS accretive). All the factors are still positive today, but the drawdown tells another story.

Let us understand how the drawdown can be explained. First of all, earnings growth has reset lower in the recent quarters. What was looking like early double-digit EPS growth in calendar year 2024 has been quietly reset to mid- to high single digits through 2025. I have plotted the quarterly diluted EPS trends (YoY) growth over the past 8 quarters below. Standalone, EPS numbers do show a lot of fluctuations, but an underlying declining trend can still be felt. What was within a still large range of 1-20% in 2024 still meant an average YoY growth of ~11-12%. That has shrunk to an average YoY growth of just above 7% in 2025. The highs are lower than 2024, and the lows are lower too.

In the backdrop of slowing EPS, what is positioned as higher than earlier guidance for Q2 (high end of low double digits) and marginally slowing for the full year (low double digits) should be seen as a reversal to earlier EPS growth rates. That is both good and bad, depending on how comparisons are made. For the immediate Q1 2026 benchmark, when ~15% adjusted EPS growth was reported, this may look like a climb down or normalization. The EPS guidance also embeds a more favorable tax rate, and the ~15% prior quarter adjusted growth had around a percentage point of FX impact boost. Arguing from the other side, for the average trends seen across 2025, this may be a strong reversal.

Another reason why growth quality is under scrutiny is that recent growth has been driven by faster-growing but structurally different segments. Value-added services (up ~28% YoY), Visa Direct transactions (up ~23% YoY), and commercial and money movement solutions (up ~20% YoY) have grown faster than payments volume (up ~8%) and cross-border business (up ~11%). This shift is creating mix-related yield pressures as flows like Visa Direct carry lower yields than traditional card transactions. Client incentives are expected to go up, and pricing benefits could moderate too, overall warranting a check on how margin expansion evolves through this shift. This does not seem to indicate significant deterioration but could be a drag on EPS growth ahead. According to management commentary -

This quarter, the one I'd note is Visa Direct, which continues to grow fast and also very profitable but typically has a lower yield than carded transactions. So to the extent Visa Direct cross-border transactions are growing faster than carded, then that's going to mix the yield down. And so that's an example of mix.

The Supporting Factors

The mix shift effect does not pose a panic threat to the Buy thesis because the current segments that are growing at lower yields are still high growth and have not impacted FCF margins meaningfully despite the drag. The core payments and cross-border growth have been strong on a standalone basis too (payments were reporting similar ~8% growth in Q2 FY2025 too, which is what I had at the time of the July thesis) - it is only the relatively higher growth in Visa Direct and other segments that are potentially pressuring overall margins to an extent (not noticeable enough to meaningfully alter EPS or FCF margin). In fact, Visa Direct was only growing at a higher rate at that time (~28%). So, the lower-yield segments are overall high growth (though moderating), while the payments and cross-border segments are broadly still intact - the net effect on EPS expectations is not materially negative.

Additionally, the fact that the current growth areas are margin dilutive also comes with the fact that they are high growth, enabling Visa to capture new TAM and presenting future monetization improvement possibilities (alongside a larger base).

The most important support for follow-up Buying comes from the valuation reset itself. The price to forward earnings has been reset to ~23x (from around 30x at the time of the earlier thesis). The free cash flow yield has moved closer to 4% from ~3% earlier. That is a rerating not commensurate with any structural deterioration.

This rerating seems to acknowledge an expectation of normalized growth ahead and is ~20% lower than the 5-year average forward earnings valuations (that are closer to mid-cycle levels). The expectations are 12-13% EPS growth over the next 2-3 years - that is close to the guidance and should actually count as a rebound from the ~7-8% growth levels on average seen in 2025. That is how I am viewing it - not a climb down from the Q1 numbers, but rather as a rebound to lower double digits (close to 2024 averages).

Even if the multiple stays depressed (at current levels), a double-digit earnings growth presents a decent return opportunity of a similar magnitude. If the risk-on sentiment normalizes, we could see some rerating toward ~25x (if not beyond 30x levels seen in 2025), - that could optionally add further upside. Hence, I retain the Buy rating on Visa overall unless there is structural and visible disruption from newer fintech plays. That is not threatened today because they seem to be using existing payment infrastructure and networks rather than challenging them. FCF and earnings data also do not show any reason to account for such disruptions in the near term.

data.title: Nomad Foods: Macro Headwinds Set To Go From Bad To Worse
data.description:
Things are not about to get easier for Nomad Foods (NOMD). After a melange of headwinds weighed heavily on the firm's operating metrics in 2025, management expects the trend to carry over into 2026, guiding to another year of declining revenue and earnings. Oh, and this guidance was delivered before the start of the war in Iran.

The market has not taken the news well. Nomad's stock has delivered a circa 18% loss since my last update in October, making a mockery of that article's "Strong Buy" rating.

There is a lot for prospective investors to chew on here. Already difficult, Nomad's operating environment could be made a lot worse by the economic fallout from events in the Middle East. Leverage is also considerable. In recognition of this, Nomad's shares now trade for less than 6x guided forward EPS. For that reason, I continue to rate shares "Strong Buy."

No Relief In 2026

To quickly recap, Nomad Foods is one of the largest frozen food players in Europe. Many of its brands, which include Birds Eye, Findus, iglo, and Goodfella's, will be household names for readers on the continent. If you're reading from elsewhere, then think frozen fish, poultry, vegetables, ice cream, and pizza.

The company has been beset by a number of headwinds: fragile consumer sentiment, cost inflation, and retail inventory destocking chief among them. Record-warm temperatures in the summer didn't help either (unsurprisingly, people typically don't want to eat fish fingers when it's very hot!).

All of this has contributed to weak revenue and even weaker earnings. While this was already apparent at the time of my last piece, things haven't improved in the interim, with Nomad's volume and organic revenue both falling by around 1% in the final quarter of 2025. On the plus side, retail sell-out was modestly positive (+0.7%) and represented an acceleration on earlier periods (full-year sell-out was 0.4%). As for earnings, with supply chain inflation amounting to a circa 350 basis points headwind, Nomad's gross margin contracted 210 basis points last year to 27.5%. On the back of this, adjusted EBITDA fell 7.5% to €523 million.

Unfortunately, things aren't slated to get better this year. Per below, revenue and profits are both expected to fall, with management only targeting a return to growth in 2027.

Of course, these numbers come with a huge caveat, which is that they were delivered just before the beginning of the war in Iran. As everyone knows, energy prices have risen sharply since then, including European gas prices, as shown below.

The outlook for consumers' budgets has surely deteriorated. To give just one example, households in the United Kingdom could be looking at a circa 20% jump in their energy bills in the summer. Nomad generates nearly 30% of its total sales in that country.

On its most recent earnings call, Nomad's CEO mentioned the following about the price gap between Nomad and its private label competitors:

The key point from our side, of course, we have a fairly meaningful differential in price versus private label.

Given competition from cheaper alternatives and the deteriorating outlook for consumer sentiment, it's clear to see how the company could be hit, and this is just from the demand side. Rising inflation would surely have a negative impact on its cost base as well. With pressure coming from both sides, the outlook for Nomad's already-squeezed profit margins doesn't look great.

High Leverage

Nomad's debt load was mentioned in the comments section of my last piece. It's true that leverage is significant here. Net of cash, the company had roughly €2 billion in conventional non-lease debt at the end of 2025—equal to around 3.75 times adjusted EBITDA for the year (even higher based on 2026 guidance).

For leverage to fall, Nomad can either grow its EBITDA or reduce its outstanding net debt. As management has already guided, the former wasn't going to be a 2026 story even before Iran. As for the latter, the good news is that Nomad does typically generate solid levels of free cash flow—around €1.75 billion over the 2019-2025 period, to be more precise. Last year's print was quite subdued, clocking in at roughly €180 million, but this was on a relatively low cash conversion ratio of 73% of adjusted net income. 2026 guidance included a 90%-plus cash conversion ratio, which is in line with the company's historical average.

It is also important to point out that virtually none of Nomad's debt is due this year. The next significant maturity isn't until 2028, when €800 million in fixed-rate senior notes is due. So, the company isn't facing any immediate refinancing risk.

The Valuation

While things still look tough for Nomad's business, its stock has also taken a beating. It has fallen 50% this past year, leaving it around 70% below its all-time 2021 high.

At $9.75, the shares trade for just 5.5 times the mid-point of 2026 EPS guidance. Here's how that stacks up next to a selection of peers from the same industry.

In fairness, a discount to peers may be warranted for all kinds of reasons. We've already established that Nomad's leverage is on the high side, for example. So, let's compare it to Nomad's own historical range. Based on its year-end stock price, it has typically traded for around 12 times adjusted EPS.

Admittedly, there is now a larger element of speculation to Nomad's investment case, given the company's macro headwinds are about to get worse. Given how fluid the driver of this deterioration is, it's very difficult to quantify the extent and duration of the firm's challenges.

Still, the potential upside from these levels is significant. Let's assume it takes Nomad until 2027 to earn what it expected to make in 2026. If the stock were to trade for just 7 times earnings, it would be 25% higher than today's price. At 8 times, it's over 40% higher. That makes it worth the risk in my view. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Strong Buy" rating in place.

data.title: Newell Brands- A Potential Turnaround Could Be Pushed Back Even Further
data.description:
Introduction

Newell Brands (NWL), a diversified consumer goods company that markets and sells its broad product portfolio (see image below to get a better sense of its product categories) in over 150 countries across the globe (although over 60% of sales are still in the U.S.) has proven to be a source of wealth destruction for investors for a while now.

For instance, over the past year, other consumer discretionary stocks have at least managed positive returns of single digits (on average), and other small caps have fared even better. But NWL, which currently has a market cap of less than $1.5B, has lost almost half its value.

Is there hope for this beleaguered entity, which houses some storied consumer brands like Rubbermaid, Graco, and Coleman (amongst others), to turn things around, or is the distressing status quo likely to persist?

Business Growth Will Likely Get Worse Before It Gets Better

If some passive observers of NWL are wondering why the stock has taken such a beating, they may want to first start with looking at the company’s topline backdrop, where it has failed to post any positive annual growth for 15 straight quarters, on the trot (that’s almost 4 years of revenue attrition)!

Against this very low bar, one would hope that NWL can eke out some positive growth soon enough, and it appears that we will finally get that this year, but not until the seasonally weakest Q1 is out of the way and potentially even Q2 (last year’s Q1, which is the base period, had also benefited from 1-2% of positive pre-buying due to impending tariff pressures further in the year). To shed more insight, note that revenue growth in Q1-26 is expected to worsen by -3 to -5% YoY (consensus is budgeting for a figure of -3.99%) from the -2.6% run rate in Q4-25.

Now the expectation in February was that Q2-26 would finally mark an inflection point in growth (see consensus expectations above), and while I don’t think Q2 will be as bad as Q1, I also suspect that the near 2% growth expectations may have to be dialed down, in light of the Middle-Eastern conflict that has snowballed in recent weeks.

NWL management previously spoke about a likely pickup in retailer shelf resets and innovation shipments (NWL has been busy rebuilding an innovation pipeline across all their key categories over 3 years) in early Q2 ahead of the spring and summer uplift, but considering a likely spike in energy costs and how that impacts household budgets, retailer shelves are unlikely to be brimming with the discretionary products that NWL specializes in.

Weak Earnings Outlook Is Reflected In Cheap Valuations, While Leverage Pressures Are Unlikely to Dim

You also have to consider that part of the likely turnaround in NWL was going to come from an expected pickup in NWL’s international business. However, important regions such as EMEA [Europe, Middle East, Africa] and APAC [Asia Pacific], which jointly account for almost one-fourth of NWL’s total business, are likely to be adversely impacted by the restrictions around the Strait of Hormuz. Not only are these regions going to be hamstrung by weak consumer discretionary appetite, but you’re also going to see a spike in petrochemical costs, as well as higher freight costs, which will end up hampering these international businesses’ operating position. NWL was previously budgeting for flattish gross margins of 34.2% (relative to FY25) with around 90bps of operating margin improvements (8.9%), but I suspect these expectations may need to be curtailed now.

Investors should also keep in mind that even this year, NWL’s US business (which is 60% of the overall business) will continue to face tariff headwinds (after all, 43% of their US portfolio is imported). Last year, this ended up impacting NWL’s profitability by $114M, or $0.23/share, and while this year, the impact will be a lot less pronounced, it is still expected to shave off $0.07 of the FY26 expected EPS. All in all, note that even though NWL may be priced at a seemingly cheap forward earnings multiple of less than 6x, that multiple is reflective of a business that is unlikely to see any earnings growth by the end of this year.

Another problematic aspect of the NWL story is the heightened level of financial gearing that this business employs. Even if one doesn’t consider the $433M of operating lease liabilities, NWL’s total financial debt amounts to a whopping $4670M, and dwarves the cash balance (of just $200M), resulting in a net debt figure of $4470M, which is higher than what was seen in FY24 ($4390M). This net debt figure basically translates to well over 5x NWL’s EBITDA base, and if we are not going to get the requisite improvement on the operating front, it is difficult to see how these leverage levels will drop to NWL management’s long-term target of 2.5x any time soon.

NWL’s Dividends Serve as a Silver Lining

It wouldn’t be entirely inappropriate to suggest that one of the prime reasons why a fair few investors are still keeping faith with NWL, or potentially even looking to pursue it now, is because of its dividend profile.

To elaborate, this is a business that has been paying stable quarterly dividends for more than two-and-a-half decades, and even though the dividends had been cut drastically in mid-2023 by 70%, those diminished dividends at the current share price work out to a pretty dazzling annualized yield of close to 8.5% (over the past 5 years, the stock’s average yield has been lower at over 5%)!

One can see how this would whet the appetite of income investors, as, out of the 500-odd consumer discretionary stocks that are covered by Seeking Alpha, a good chunk doesn’t even pay dividends, and amongst those who do, the average yield only works out to a little over 3.2%. Also consider that the most popular high-yielding equity ETF, Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), of which NWL is one of 560-odd names, only offers a sub-par yield of 2.41%. In light of this scenario, some would argue that NWL’s relative strength ratio (vs its peers from VYM) deserves to be at record lows, making it a potential candidate for upward mean reversion.

I recognize that some investors may have some trepidation over the coverage and sustainability of those dividends, considering NWL’s less-than-ideal financial position, but I believe they should still generate ample enough free cash flow [FCF] to cover those dividends.

At $0.07 per quarter, the annualized cash outlay for NWL’s dividends works out to less than $120M, but this year NWL looks set to grow its operating cash flow base by 40% (driven by a tighter working capital load, and lower taxes and bonuses, if not for mid-single-digit EBTIDA growth), and trim its CAPEX commitments by $200M, which should result in positive free cash of $170M-$175M (last year’s FCF was just $17M). This cadence of FCF could cover the dividends by 1.42x and still leave around $50M that could be used elsewhere.

Technicals- Strong Bearish Pressure, While an Impending Test of a Pivot Low Could Be Key

NWL’s valuation multiple may come across as dirt-cheap, and its yield may come across as eye-popping, but considering the pronounced bearish pressure that is evident on NWL’s charts, I don’t feel an investment now would be the most sensible decision.

On the daily time frame, NWL is now encountering meaningful selling pressure, with a series of lower lows and lower highs since mid-February. One can also see that it is trading below all its key moving averages, and its intermediate peak in mid-February, coincided with its inability to clear the psychological barrier of the 200DMA (something which it hasn’t managed to do for 13 months now). The gap with the 200DMA has since expanded to a meaningful threshold of 28% highlighting how strong the bearish momentum is.

The next crucial test will be to see if NWL will revisit its pivot low of $3.07 that was seen in November 2025 and potentially build a base there, or will it just slide even further (the former outlook could be seen as an encouraging sign). For now, I would advise prospective investors to just stand by and watch.

Closing Thoughts

Newell Brands, which was expected to see a turnaround (at least in topline growth prospects) by Q2-26, is likely to face a longer recovery period, and the stock’s slide in recent weeks reflects this. While the stock’s valuations remain cheap, investors buying now will be buying a business that is set to witness a contraction in earnings by the end of this year. Having said that, NWL’s eye-popping dividend yield may prompt some additional interest from yield-chasing investors, more so as the dividend is likely to be well covered by ample free cash flow this year. All in all, we feel a hold rating looks appropriate now.

data.title: Eli Lilly's Centessa Deal Shows It Is Awake To New Narcolepsy Reality
data.description:
Investment Overview

News has broken this morning that Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), the world's largest Pharmaceutical company by market cap valuation - presently $837bn (down from >$1 trillion dollars earlier this year), is set to acquire Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc (CNTA) in a deal valued at ~$6.3bn, or $38 per share, with a contingent value right ("CVR") attached that could provide an additional potential aggregate equity value of ~$1.5 billion, or $9 per share.

In a press release issued today, Centessa discusses its business as follows:

Centessa is advancing a pipeline of orexin receptor 2 (OX2R) agonists designed to address the neurobiological system critical to the sleep-wake cycle to treat excessive daytime sleepiness and disorders of impaired wakefulness.

Its lead investigational candidate cleminorexton (formerly ORX750) has demonstrated a potential best-in-class profile in Phase 2a clinical studies across narcolepsy type 1, narcolepsy type 2, and idiopathic hypersomnia.

Centessa's OX2R agonist portfolio includes additional clinical and preclinical-stage assets with potential utility across a broader range of neurological, neurodegenerative, and neuropsychiatric conditions.

Analysis - Why Centessa, Why Now?

Eli Lilly stock is up >1,100% on a 10-year basis, and >375% on a five-year basis - phenomenal returns for a "Big Pharma" company and primarily built on the runaway success of its drug molecule tirzepatide, a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetic, approved to treat Type 2 diabetes as Mounjaro, and Obesity as Zepbound.

These two products earned a combined ~$37bn last year - more than any other drug molecule - however Lilly also boasts a neuroscience division which earned revenues of ~$1.4bn last year, thanks to products such as depression med Cymbalta - $63m revenues in 2025, migraine therapies Emgality, $748m, and Reywow, $38m, plus Alzheimer's therapy Kisunla - $249m revenues last year.

With Cymbalta patents having expired (the product earned >$750m revenues in 2020), and the launch of Kisunla, a somewhat controversial Alzheimer's therapy, having not gone to plan, arguably, this division needed strengthening - Lilly's other divisions, cardiometabolic, Oncology, and Immunology earned revenues of $48.2bn, $9.4bn, and $5.2bn last year.

As we can see above (source: Centessa February corporate presentation) none of Centessa's pipeline assets have made it into a pivotal clinical study as yet, so Lilly will not be getting any immediate revenue boost from this acquisition, but the company may be playing an intelligent long game.

Centessa's lead candidate is clearly ORX750, which is indicated for narcolepsy types 1 and 2, and idiopathic hypersomnia, a rare neurological disorder that Centessa's research suggests affects ~65k Americans.

Centessa states in its 2024 annual report / 10-K filing that:

While prevailing treatment approaches may address some of the symptoms of NT1, there are no currently approved therapies that address the loss of orexin.

The current treatment paradigm for narcolepsy typically involves a polypharmacy approach aimed to address the most predominant symptoms, which are EDS (excessive daytime sleepiness) and cataplexy.

There are now four treatments approved to treat both EDS and cataplexy, which are LUMRYZ (extended-release sodium oxybate) from Avadel Pharmaceuticals plc, WAKIX (pitolisant) marketed by Harmony Biosciences (HRMY), and XYREM® (sodium oxybate) and XYWAV® (calcium oxybate; magnesium oxybate; potassium oxybate; sodium oxybate) marketed by Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ)

In 2025, Xywav earned revenues of ~$1.7bn, Lumryz ~$280m, and Wakiz ~$870m, underlining the size of the market opportunity that may await ORX750, if it can be successfully negotiated through a pivotal study and the regulatory submission process.

Last November, Centessa shared Phase 2a study data pertaining to ORX750 in NT1, as follows:

  • Statistically significant, clinically meaningful and dose-dependent improvements from baseline compared with placebo in mean sleep latency on the MWT at both doses. In the 1.5 mg cohort (n=6), ORX750 achieved a >20 minute change from baseline in mean sleep latency compared with placebo on the MWT at Week 2 (p-value =0.0026), with half the participants achieving >30 minutes in mean sleep latency on the MWT. Dose escalation is progressing with both once-daily and split-dose regimens.
  • Statistically significant, clinically meaningful and dose-dependent improvements from baseline in ESS total score compared with placebo at both doses. In the 1.5 mg cohort (n=7), participants had a mean ESS total score of 5.1 with ORX750 compared to a mean ESS total score of 18.7 with placebo at Week 2 (p-value =0.0001). Participants had a mean ESS total score of 19.6 at baseline.
  • Statistically significant, clinically meaningful and dose-dependent reductions in Weekly Cataplexy Rate (WCR) at both doses. In the 1.5 mg cohort (n=7), participants with ORX750 had an 87% relative reduction in WCR compared with placebo, with an estimated incidence rate ratio of 0.13 at Week 2 (p-value = 0.0025).

In its press release today, Eli Lilly suggested the data gives OXR750 "a potential best-in-class profile, however, we may note that Centessa is not the only company developing such an asset.

Last November, Alkermes - which completed the acquisition of Avadel last October, in a deal valued $2.1bn - shares the news that its orexin 2 receptor ("OX2R") agonist alixorexton had met dual efficacy endpoints in a Phase 2 study, showing:

statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements from baseline compared to placebo on the Maintenance of Wakefulness Test ("MWT") and Epworth Sleepiness Scale ("ESS").

Furthermore, Japanese Pharma Takeda has successfully advanced its OX2R candidate oveporexton through a Phase 3 study, and the drug is now under priority review by the FDA, and could be approved in Q3 of this year.

Analysts have apparently forecast 2031 sales of oveporexton of ~$1.3bn, with OXR750 expected to earn $875m of revenues in the same year, and Alkermes' alixorexton ~$780m.

Analysis: A Deal That Will Suit Both Parties, But Is Not Without Risk For Eli Lilly

Regarding the contingent value rights ("CVRs") Lilly states in today's press release:

CVR holders would become entitled to receive contingent payments as follows: (i) $2.00 per CVR in cash, upon U.S. FDA approval of cleminorexton (formerly ORX750) or ORX142 for the treatment of narcolepsy type 2 prior to the fifth anniversary of transaction closing; (ii) $5.00 per CVR in cash, upon U.S. FDA approval of cleminorexton (formerly ORX750) or ORX142 for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia prior to the fifth anniversary of transaction closing; and (iii) $2.00 per CVR in cash, upon the first U.S. FDA approval of cleminorexton (formerly ORX750) or ORX142 for the treatment of any indication prior to January 1, 2030. There can be no assurance that any payments will be made with respect to the CVR.

Data shared to date by three different pharma companies certainly seems to suggest that OX2R agonists may be the future of the narcolepsy industry - for example, being potentially safer, with fewer side-effects than sodium oxybate, and working in a very different way to Wakix, which promotes feelings of wakefulness as opposed to targeting orexin, a key signaling peptide in the brain.

Research suggests Physicians may be willing to switch more than half of their narcolepsy patients onto OX2R agonists, creating a potential $5bn market, which suggests that a 20% market share could make OXR750, or cleminorexton to give it its chemical name, a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) drug for many years, potentially justifying the premium price Lilly has paid for Centessa.

Still, there is a caveat to the deal in the sense that cleminorexton has not completed a pivotal study - one was expected to begin this quarter in NT1, 2 and IH - which means that there is still a risk that the asset could fail to meet endpoints in such a study, or meet them, but show inferiority to either Alkermes or Takeda's candidates - and we may note that Jazz Pharma, Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM), NLS Pharma and Japanese Pharma Eisai are also developing assets using this same MoA.

Naturally, Eli Lilly will have done its due diligence, and it has paid substantially more than Alkermes paid to acquire Avadel, so it must be very confident in the profile of not just cleminorexton, but the earlier stage assets also, which may target related neurological indications, enhancing the overall market opportunity.

Concluding Thoughts - Does Today's Deal Make Eli Lilly / Centessa Stock More Attractive?

At present, Centessa stock trades at a value of $40 per share, so anyone buying stock today could realize a gain on their investment if the CVR conditions are met, which seems reasonably likely.

If Lilly is able to share successful pivotal study data this year - which seems possible, as the Phase 2a study assessed patients at the end of 2 weeks - then we may speculate it could submit a New Drug Application before the end of Q1 2027, and if using a priority review voucher, or qualifying for the FDA's fast-track review programs, shortening the review period from 10 months to six months or less, cleminorexton could be on the market by late 2027.

If buying today, then, investors could realize a~20% return on their investment if CVR conditions are met, albeit, the wait to "cash in" could be 18 months or more, and they may not be paid out at all, if there is a study setback.

From Eli Lilly's perspective, the company is forecasting for 25% revenue growth in 2026, to $80bn - $84bn of revenues, and earnings per share of $33.5 - $35. As such, the peak revenue potential of the Centessa OX2R portfolio - let's assume $1.5bn - represents >2% of Lilly's 2026 revenue target, so this deal is not necessarily going to set Lilly's share price alight.

Nevertheless, for management I believe it ticks the right boxes, positioning the company to contest for market share, within a couple of years perhaps, in a ~$5bn market, reviving the neuroscience division, and maintaining the company's reputation for being an all-court Pharma giant, not just an obesity / T2D company.

A few days ago, Lilly also struck a deal with Insilico Medicine to use Insilico's AI engine to help develop novel drug candidates, while it has also agreed a deal to partner with semi-conductor giant Nvidia (NVDA) and invest up to $1 billion in an AI co-innovation lab.

In 2025, the Pharma earned net income of ~$20.6bn, nearly twice what it earned in 2024, which was itself twice the figure it earned in 2023. That puts Lilly in the luxurious position of being able to commit large sums to strategic M&A (in fact, this may result in the Pharma being forced to overpay for these types of deals, with the market aware of its excellent cash flow capabilities) and the Pharma is clearly committed to "fixing the roof while the sun is shining," i.e., making deals today that will help generate potentially multibillions of dollars of revenues in the years ahead.

While the fact that as many as five companies are planning to contest the emerging OX2R / narcolepsy / IH market adds an element of risk to today's deal, Lilly's global infrastructure, manufacturing, and marketing capabilities are arguably second to none, which may put it in the box seat should it successfully bring cleminorexton to market, which I suspect is more likely than not.

As such, I can see the logic of being invested in both Lilly and Centessa today, and would assign both stocks a Buy rating.

data.title: Revisiting Energy Market Impacts From The Iran War
data.description:
By Stacey Morris, CFA

As oil looks poised to notch its largest monthly gain ever amid the war in Iran, energy stocks have been the main bright spot in a tough tape, leading all other sectors by a landslide. To be fair, the energy benchmark was already up 25% before the war began and oil prices skyrocketed. Year-to-date through March 27, energy is up just over 40% on a total return basis, while the S&P 500 is down 6.7% for the period. Following a month of war, it is time to revisit the implications for energy markets.

The key variables for energy discussed at the onset of the war were: 1) how long the Strait of Hormuz would be closed; and 2) how much damage would be done to energy infrastructure in the region. The outcomes for both have likely been worse than initially feared. This note discusses some of the key developments in liquefied natural gas (LNG), oil, and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) markets in recent weeks, as well as the medium-term implications of the war for North American midstream.

Liquefied Natural Gas: Qatari Exports Possibly Hampered for 3-5 Years

While the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has restricted LNG flows, Iranian attacks on a key export facility in Qatar are set to have longer-term impacts. According to reports, the damage will take 3–5 years to repair, keeping 12.8 million tons per annum (MTPA) or ~1.7 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) offline. Qatar is the second-largest LNG exporter globally, and the damaged assets represent 17% of its capacity. For context, the U.S. exported 14.6 Bcf/d in 2025 (read more).

The disruption to LNG flows and asset damage ultimately makes North American LNG relatively more attractive for global buyers in the long run. That stands to benefit established players like Cheniere (LNG) and Venture Global (VG) that can continue to add capacity over time. Cheniere and VG each have potential expansions of over 40 MTPA and 30 MTPA, respectively, that have not yet reached final investment decision, or FID (i.e., started construction). The current backdrop also provides momentum to projects trying to secure financing, finalize sales agreements, and reach FID.

VG recently announced five-year LNG purchase agreements with both Trafigura at 0.5 MTPA on March 2, and Vitol at 1.5 MTPA on March 23. Deals with both trading companies will start this year, with Trafigura’s starting in 2Q. VG was 69% contracted for its expected 2026 cargos as of February 25. VG also greenlit Phase 2 of its CP2 LNG facility in mid-March, closing $8.6 billion of related project financing for the 9-MTPA expansion.

Cheniere typically has over 90% of its capacity secured under long-term contracts, limiting its ability to sell spot cargos. However, at the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston last week, Cheniere’s CEO reportedly said the company’s fifth train of the Corpus Christi Stage 3 project may be operational on March 27. That train first produced LNG in February. The company is also examining its maintenance schedule to try to send more cargos.

The chart below shows European and Asian benchmark LNG prices over the last month relative to the U.S. benchmark Henry Hub, which has seen no real impact. For LNG importers, the disruptions and price spikes raise questions around energy security and reliability.

In Oil Markets, Brent Crude Sees More Severe Price Impact

While refineries and infrastructure in the region have been damaged, the impact on oil likely moderates once the Strait is reopened and fighting eases. That said, oil supplies globally are down over 10 million barrels per day (MMBpd) since the start of the war, and inventories are declining. The global, seaborne benchmark Brent crude has seen the brunt of the increase, as discussed below. Oil price curves remain extremely backwardated, with the May contract trading more than $10 per barrel above the July contract for both Brent and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) at writing. Looking further out, the curves for both have shifted higher through the end of the decade.

The politics of prices at the pump in an election year have driven an emphasis in the U.S. on lowering oil prices, but measures have been taken globally to increase supplies. Namely, the International Energy Agency announced a 400-million barrel (MMBbl) release from emergency reserves, with the U.S. contributing 172 MMBbls. The U.S. has even temporarily lifted sanctions on Iranian and Russian oil at sea - a move that will add an estimated 140 MMBbls of Iranian oil and 124 MMBbls of Russian oil to the market, albeit volumes are difficult to ascertain.

For its part, Saudi Aramco (ARMCO) has reportedly ramped up volumes through its East-West pipeline, which flows to Yanbu on the Red Sea. The pipeline has been reported to temporarily move up to 7 MMBpd in the past. Yanbu exports were estimated just above 4 MMBpd in mid-March, providing some relief.

Supply disruptions have had a greater impact on Brent as the global oil benchmark. As shown below, Brent was trading $13.53 per barrel above the U.S. benchmark WTI as of March 26. Even WTI at Houston, which is a better seaborne comparison, was trading more than $10 below Brent. Wide spreads between Brent and WTI were common during the early 2010s as significant growth in U.S. production put downward pressure on WTI and barrels were constrained getting from the Cushing oil hub to markets (namely the Gulf Coast). The widened Brent-WTI spread tends to mainly benefit U.S. refiners, which can enjoy a feedstock cost advantage while still selling refined products that tend to be more reflective of global oil prices.

Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Also Impacted

In addition to being a major LNG exporter, Qatar is also known for its exports of condensate, which could be described as very light crude, and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), which includes propane and butane. Attacks on infrastructure are expected to curb Qatar’s exports of condensate by a fourth and LPG exports by 13%. The U.S. is a major producer and exporter of condensate and LPG. Similar to LNG, global buyers may be more interested in inking long-term contracts with U.S. suppliers. Within U.S. midstream, Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Targa Resources (TRGP) currently operate LPG export capacity, while MPLX (MPLX) and ONEOK (OKE) are partnering on an LPG export terminal set to come on-line in 2028.

Additional Energy Implications

Coming into the year, the consensus was cautious on oil prices due to oversupply concerns. This also led to some worries around U.S. production trends and the impact to midstream. The outlook has quickly reversed. In its March Short-Term Energy Outlook, the U.S. Energy Information Administration raised its production forecasts for 2027 for oil, natural gas, and NGLs. The 2027 oil forecast was increased by 0.5 MMBpd to 13.83 MMBpd, which would be a new record. While a focus on capital discipline likely keeps production growth moderate, fears of oil production flatlining or falling have been eased with higher prices.

Looking more broadly, the U.S. and Canada are likely to be even more attractive partners for energy importers going forward, particularly for LNG and LPG. The proximity of Canada’s west coast export infrastructure to Asian markets is a key advantage, albeit Canadian export capacity is limited (read more).

Similar to the aftereffects of the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, energy security, reliability, and affordability are likely to remain topical. Countries with diversified energy suppliers and a mix of energy sources are better positioned to weather energy market disruptions. A more robust power mix includes natural gas, renewables, coal, and nuclear.

It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
